target: Overview scorecard (sections.json part1) + Evaluation Guide (generate-html.js) — ISS-767, ISS-768, ISS-771
summary: Three surgical patches to close the review's single biggest rhetorical vulnerability: a dome defender can say "your taxonomy has no success category — the review is itself unfalsifiable." The curmudgeon rated this 8.5/10 as the most effective rebuttal. These patches neutralize it by (1) reframing the scorecard as a summary of findings, (2) adding an explicit "What Would Change Our Verdict" section, and (3) clarifying what Std Model Explains does and doesn't mean.
kernel_of_truth: The curmudgeon is right that a 0/N blanket dismissal invites legitimate suspicion of reviewer bias. Any review finding zero merit in N claims should earn that conclusion explicitly. The review's Evaluation Guide already contains the intellectual apparatus (discriminating prediction standard, medicine analogy) but it's buried on Tab 2 and never connected to the scorecard on Tab 1. The dome defender's taxonomy critique has genuine philosophical force — a framework with no success category IS structurally unfalsifiable, regardless of whether each individual verdict is correct. Our fix: make the review explicitly falsifiable by stating concrete conditions that would change verdicts.

patches.target_location: part1 HTML — scorecard div, the sc-card accent (0 Survive Scrutiny card)
patches.description: Reframe the 0-card from a declaration to a finding. Add qualifying sublabel acknowledging real observations.
patches.current_html:
0
Survive Scrutiny
Every claim is refuted, self-contradicted, explained by standard physics, misleading, undemonstrated, or unfalsifiable
patches.replacement_html:
0
Survive Scrutiny
After reviewing each claim against published data and the model's own equations, none produces a prediction that both derives from dome geometry and differs from standard physics. Many referenced observations are real — the verdicts address interpretation, not the data. What would change this?
patches.rationale: The current sublabel reads as a predetermined conclusion ("every claim IS..."). The replacement frames it as a finding ("after reviewing... none produces...") and links directly to the falsifiability conditions. The acknowledgment that observations are real preempts the "you dismiss everything" attack. The cross-link to the Evaluation Guide makes the methodology immediately accessible.

patches.target_location: part1 HTML — immediately before the scorecard div (before <div class="scorecard")
patches.description: Add a one-sentence framing paragraph above the scorecard.
patches.insertion_html: Summary of findings — the evidence supporting each verdict is presented in the tabs that follow. Our criteria for what would change these verdicts are stated in the Evaluation Guide.
patches.rationale: This single sentence transforms the scorecard from a conclusion-before-evidence into a transparent summary that points readers to methodology. It preempts the "verdict before trial" critique without removing the scorecard's impact.

patches.target_location: Evaluation Guide tab — new <details> section after the eg-ai </details> block (line ~1387), before the sectionNav call (line ~1389)
patches.description: Add a "What Would Change Our Verdict" section — the review's explicit falsifiability conditions.
patches.replacement_html:
What Would Change Our Verdict

This review is falsifiable. Four concrete conditions would cause us to upgrade a verdict — any one is sufficient. Of the current claims, only 4 even attempt condition 1, and all 4 produce values that match standard physics or fail against measurement.

A review that finds zero valid predictions in a set of ${counts.total} should invite scrutiny. We agree. Here are four concrete, independently verifiable conditions under which we would upgrade a verdict. Any single condition is sufficient:

Condition 1: Derive a discriminating prediction from dome geometry. Use the model's own stated parameters — H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619), disc radius 20,015 km, κ = 1.67 nT/μGal — to derive a quantitative prediction that differs from what standard physics predicts for the same observable. Then confirm it with independent measurement. Currently only 4 of ${counts.total} claims even attempt a dome-geometry derivation (WIN-020, WIN-044, WIN-057, WIN-058), and all four produce values that either match standard physics or contradict measurement.

Condition 2: Produce a genuine prospective prediction. Register a specific quantitative prediction (value ± uncertainty, named observable, named measurement station) before the confirming data exists, with the prediction document independently timestamped separately from any observation data. The dome's August 2026 eclipse predictions approach this standard but have methodological issues documented in our Timestamp Error analysis.

Condition 3: Derive 7.83 Hz from dome geometry. The Schumann fundamental resonance at 7.83 Hz is one of the most precisely measured electromagnetic constants on Earth. The dome's own cavity geometry — an exponentially decaying firmament over a flat disc — predicts a fundamental frequency of approximately 22 Hz when the stated dimensions are used honestly. If someone can derive 7.83 Hz from dome parameters without smuggling in the globe's radius (6,371 km) or its spherical cavity eigenvalue formula, that would be a genuine dome prediction that standard physics cannot replicate.

Condition 4: Produce a coordinate system that achieves sub-5% error without globe inputs. The dome's V12/V13 coordinate system claims impressive accuracy for city-to-city distances, but takes WGS84 (globe) coordinates as input and curve-fits against globe-measured distances. If a coordinate system built entirely from dome geometry — disc radius, azimuthal distance from the north pole, no latitude/longitude conversions — could reproduce real-world distances to comparable accuracy, that would constitute independent evidence for the dome's geometry.

These conditions are demanding but not impossible. They are the same standard applied in all of science: show that your model predicts something the alternatives do not, and confirm it with data. We are not asking the dome to solve every problem — science is iterative, and a single discriminating prediction would be a genuine breakthrough worth acknowledging. The reason the current scorecard shows zero is not that we constructed an impossible standard — it is that none of the ${counts.total} claims meets even the weakest version of this standard.

Eclipse pre-commitment: The dome model has registered predictions for the August 12, 2026 annular solar eclipse: magnetic anomalies of −17 to −21 nT at specified stations. Based on the Chapman mechanism (the standard physics explanation for eclipse-induced magnetic effects, documented since 1933), we expect anomalies of 5–20 nT in magnitude. If the dome's predictions are confirmed and fall outside the Chapman-mechanism range — that is, if the measured values match the dome's −17 to −21 nT range but not the 5–20 nT Chapman range — we will upgrade the relevant WIN. If they fall within both ranges, the result supports standard physics, not dome geometry, because the dome's prediction values were derived by scaling Chapman-mechanism data. We state this now, before the eclipse, so there is no ambiguity afterward.
patches.rationale: This is the single most important addition. It makes the review explicitly falsifiable — the dome defender cannot claim "no outcome you would accept" when four concrete acceptance conditions are stated and cross-linked from the scorecard. The eclipse pre-commitment closes the "wait and see" strategy by stating the discriminating criteria in advance. The acknowledgment ("A review that finds zero valid predictions... should invite scrutiny. We agree.") disarms the rhetoric without conceding the substance.

patches.target_location: Evaluation Guide tab — inside eg-evaluate details, after the medicine analogy paragraph (after the paragraph ending '...not evidence for the dome.', approximately line 1369), before the 'Can the prediction be derived' paragraph
patches.description: Add a clarification of what "Std Model Explains" means and does not mean.
patches.insertion_html: A note on "Standard Model Explains" verdicts. This verdict does not mean the observation is wrong or the dome's numerical value is incorrect. It means the observation was already predicted and explained by standard physics before the dome model existed. ${counts.stdModel} claims receive this verdict. In many cases, the dome model correctly identifies a real phenomenon — magnetic pole acceleration, tidal periodicity, Schumann resonance stability. We acknowledge these as genuine observations. The verdict addresses attribution: the dome claims these observations as evidence for its geometry, but the quantitative derivation comes entirely from standard physics. Reproducing known results is necessary for any theory (a flat-earth model that could not account for tides would be immediately falsified) but it is not sufficient evidence for the model's specific claims about geometry. The medicine analogy above applies: correctly predicting that a cut heals is expected of any medical theory and does not distinguish between them.
patches.rationale: This directly addresses ISS-771. The current "Std Model Explains" verdict is the most rhetorically vulnerable category because it punishes the dome for agreeing with reality. The clarification reframes it as a statement about attribution and discriminating power, not about correctness. It acknowledges the dome's observations are real (preempting the "you dismiss everything" attack) while explaining why non-discriminating predictions don't constitute evidence for the specific geometry.

holes_addressed: ISS-767: Overview scorecard appears to prejudge its subject — reframed as summary of findings with cross-link to methodology
ISS-768: No explicit success category / review is unfalsifiable — four concrete falsifiability conditions stated, eclipse pre-commitment added
ISS-771: Std Model Explains conflates non-discriminating with wrong — clarification paragraph added distinguishing attribution from correctness
new_evidence_added: Eclipse pre-commitment: 5-20 nT Chapman range vs -17 to -21 nT dome range as discriminating criteria
Enumeration of 4 WINs that attempt dome-geometry derivation (WIN-020, 044, 057, 058)
Schumann 22 Hz derivation as concrete falsifiability challenge
V12/V13 coordinate system globe-input dependency as concrete falsifiability challenge

anticipated_objections.objection: Stating falsifiability conditions gives the dome a roadmap to construct a fake success
anticipated_objections.response: All four conditions require independent measurement or mathematical derivation that cannot be faked. A genuine 7.83 Hz derivation from dome geometry would be verifiable by anyone. A discriminating eclipse prediction would be confirmed by public INTERMAGNET data. Stating the conditions openly is a sign of confidence, not vulnerability — if the dome could meet any of these, the review should acknowledge it.

anticipated_objections.objection: The eclipse pre-commitment range (5-20 nT Chapman) overlaps with the dome range (-17 to -21 nT), so any result confirms both
anticipated_objections.response: This is addressed in the text. If the measured values fall in the overlap region, that supports standard physics (which predicted the range without dome geometry). Only values outside the Chapman range but inside the dome range would discriminate. The asymmetry is honest: the dome made a specific prediction, standard physics has a broader range, and the dome range was derived from Chapman data.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding this section makes the review look defensive — like it is responding to criticism rather than presenting science
anticipated_objections.response: The curmudgeon rated the taxonomy critique 8.5/10 as the most effective rebuttal. Ignoring it is worse than addressing it. The section is written in confident, forward-looking language ("we agree this deserves scrutiny") rather than defensive language. Pre-empting the strongest counter-argument is standard scientific practice (every Nature paper has a limitations section).

anticipated_objections.objection: The scorecard intro paragraph slows down the impact — readers want the number, not a qualification
anticipated_objections.response: The framing paragraph is one sentence in italics. It adds ~3 seconds of reading time. The cost is minimal; the benefit is neutralizing the most effective dome-defender argument before it forms. A/B testing this is outside scope, but the curmudgeon's analysis suggests the unqualified scorecard is actively harmful to credibility.

implementation_notes: This expansion modifies TWO files: sections.json (scorecard patches P1-P2) and generate-html.js (Evaluation Guide patches P3-P4). The decider should apply them in order. P3 (the falsifiability section) is the highest priority — it alone closes 80% of the vulnerability. P1+P2 are complementary but lower priority. P4 is a clarity improvement. All patches use existing CSS classes (ps-summary, ps-tldr, ps-detail, scorecard-framing) — the scorecard-framing class may need to be added to the stylesheet (suggest: font-size 0.9em, text-align center, margin-bottom 0.5em, color #666). Note: P3 uses template literals (${counts.total}, ${counts.stdModel}) that are already available in generate-html.js's template context.
